summary:
You are given the root of a binary search tree and two of its node values, p and q. Return the lowest common ancestor of the two nodes.

The lowest common ancestor is the deepest node that has both p and q somewhere in its subtree. A node is allowed to be an ancestor of itself. You can exploit the BST ordering to find it efficiently.

examples.explanation: 2 lies in the left subtree and 8 in the right subtree of 6, so the root node 6 is their lowest common ancestor.
4 is a descendant of 2, so 2 is itself the lowest common ancestor.

constraints: The number of nodes is in the range [2, 10^5].
All node values are unique, and p and q are distinct values both present in the tree.
The input tree is a valid binary search tree.
